items.q: How do alkenes typically react with bromine?
items.correct: By electrophilic addition — bromine adds across the double bond to form a dibromoalkane.
items.misconception: Bromine substitutes for a hydrogen on the alkene, releasing HBr.
items.random_wrong: The alkene polymerises with bromine to form a long chain.
items.why: Students see 'reaction with halogen' and default to the substitution they learned for alkanes, not recognising that the C=C changes the mechanism.

items.q: What is the product of mild oxidation of a primary alcohol?
items.correct: An aldehyde (which can be further oxidised to a carboxylic acid).
items.misconception: A primary alcohol is oxidised directly to a ketone.
items.random_wrong: A primary alcohol is oxidised to an alkene by losing water.
items.why: Students confuse the products of primary vs secondary alcohol oxidation because they look structurally similar, but only secondary alcohols form ketones.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.
It's CN⁻. “H⁺” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Members of a homologous series share:
items.correct: The same functional group and general formula
items.why: Correct answer: The same functional group and general formula. “The same molecular mass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
